elements.sent-2411-001: The ability to explore efficiently and effectively is a central challenge of reinforcement learning.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we consider exploration through the lens of information theory.
elements.sent-2411-003: Specifically, we cast exploration as a problem of maximizing the Shannon entropy of the state occupation measure.
elements.sent-2411-004: This is done by maximizing a sequence of divergences between distributions representing an agent's past behavior and its current behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: Intuitively, this encourages the agent to explore new behaviors that are distinct from past behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-006: Hence, we call our method RAMP, for ``$R$unning $A$way fro$m$ the $P$ast.'' A fundamental question of this method is the quantification of the distribution change over time.
elements.sent-2411-007: We consider both the Kullback-Leibler divergence and the Wasserstein distance to quantify divergence between successive state occupation measures, and explain why the former might lead to undesirable exploratory behaviors in some tasks.
elements.sent-2411-008: We demonstrate that by encouraging the agent to explore by actively distancing itself from past experiences, it can effectively explore mazes and a wide range of behaviors on robotic manipulation and locomotion tasks.
elements.sent-2411-009: Exploration is essential in reinforcement learning (RL) as it allows agents to discover optimal strategies in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-010: Without it, agents risk becoming stuck in suboptimal policies, lacking the diverse experiences needed to learn effectively.
elements.sent-2411-011: Despite a long history of study, with fundamental algorithms like R-max \citep{brafman2002r , UCRL \citep{auer2006logarithmic , and E3 \citep{kearns2002near , exploration remains a major challenge in modern RL.}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: One method for encouraging exploration is providing intrinsic motivation to the agent; our approach falls into this category.
elements.sent-2411-013: The agent aims to maximize this additional (intrinsic) reward which motivates exploration.
elements.sent-2411-014: Various intrinsic reward models specific to exploration have been developed \citep{brafman2002r, auer2006logarithmic, bellemare2016unifying,eysenbach2018diversity,badia2020never .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Amongst these are methods based on the principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty \citep{munos2014bandits , where agents are encouraged to explore under-visited areas of the state space by assigning high reward values to uncertain states.}
elements.sent-2411-016: While these methods apply well to tabular state spaces, in high-dimensional or continuous state spaces, these methods must rely on parameterized functions to represent uncertainty, which may result in inconsistent behavior \citep{pathak2017curiosity, burda2018exploration .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Information theory provides a useful perspective on exploration.
elements.sent-2411-018: For example, one can define a finite set of skills which have some descriptor per skill.
elements.sent-2411-019: Maximizing the Mutual Information (MI) between the descriptors of these skills, and the states which they visit, yields good exploratory behaviors for this set of skills \citep{eysenbach2018diversity .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Similarly, it has been demonstrated that maximizing the Shannon entropy of the state distribution encourages exploration \citep{liu2021behavior,hazan2019provably, lee2019efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-021: However, these approaches often rely on probability density estimators, and finding a relevant density estimator for an environment can be challenging.
elements.sent-2411-022: This study introduces a method for exploration that aims at achieving a high Shannon entropy score of the distribution representing the agent's experiences.
elements.sent-2411-023: We show that this objective can be reframed as the maximization of a sequence of Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergences between successive state occupation measures.
elements.sent-2411-024: This new objective leads to the intuitive use of a simple classifier as a density estimator.
elements.sent-2411-025: In other words, the goal of exploration, represented by high Shannon entropy over the agent's experiences, can be achieved by iteratively separating an agent's past experiences with its most recent ones.
elements.sent-2411-026: This results in an algorithm called RAMP, for ``Running Away from the Past,'' as the agent explores by distancing itself from its past experiences.
elements.sent-2411-027: We evaluate the performance of RAMP using multiple metrics, including the state space coverage and the maximum score achieved by a policy trained to maximize a specific reward model.
elements.sent-2411-028: In environments where exploration could benefit from a metric relevant to the state space, we suggest using the Wasserstein distance as an alternative to the KL divergence.
elements.sent-2411-029: We study the difference between these two measures by proposing two versions of the algorithm: $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightblue {\text{KL }$, which uses KL divergence, and $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightcoral {\mathcal{W }$, which uses Wasserstein distance.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-030: We compare RAMP to several baselines developed for exploration tasks and across various environments such as mazes, locomotion tasks, and robotics tasks.
elements.sent-2411-031: We find that RAMP is competitive in exploration to state-of-the-art methods such as LSD \citep{park2022lipschitz on robotics tasks, and that it leads to more effective exploration on maze and locomotion tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-032: We conclude that this reformulation of the Shannon entropy objective can open further avenues in the domain of exploration in RL.
elements.sent-2411-033: All our implementations are available at the following repository: \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Exploration_by_running_away_from_the_past_039B {GitHub repository .}}

elements.sent-2411-039: Given a parameter $\beta \in (0,1)$, let $\mu_n$ be the $(1-\beta)$-discounted mixture of past state occupancies, up to epoch $n$: \begin{equation}\mu_n(s) = \beta\sum_{k=1}^{n}(1-\beta)^{n-k}\rho_k(s) \mu_{n+1}(s) = \beta\underbrace{\rho_{n+1}(s)}_{The present} + (1-\beta)\underbrace{\mu_n(s)}_{The past}\end{equation} Consider epoch $n+1$, $\rho_{n+1}$ denotes the agent's current occupancy measure density, representing its present behavior, while $\mu_n$ reflects its past experience.
elements.sent-2411-040: In practice, a sample of $\mu_n$ can be maintained using a replay buffer.

elements.sent-2411-062: The Wasserstein distance \citep{villani2009optimal , also known as the Earth Mover's Distance, provides a principled way of measuring differences between distributions by considering the cost of transporting mass between states, thereby encouraging exploration that covers distinct regions of the environment.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The Wasserstein distance is in itself an optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-064: In our case, it can be defined through the Kantorovich duality as: \begin{equation}\label{def:wasserstein} \mathcal{W}(\rho^{\pi}, \beta\rho^{\pi} + (1-\beta)\mu_{n}) = \underset{\parallel f\parallel\leq 1}{\max} \; \underset{s^{+}\sim \rho^{\pi}}{\mathbb{E}} \left[f(s^{+})\right] - \underset{s^{-}\sim \beta\rho^{\pi} + (1-\beta)\mu_{n}}{\mathbb{E}} \left[f(s^{-})\right],\end{equation} where $f$ is a 1-Lipshitz function for a given metric $d$ defined over $\mathcal{S}$: $\forall (s_1,s_2) \in \mathcal{S ^{2 \parallel f(s_2) - f(s_1) \parallel \leq d(s_1,s_2) $.}}

elements.sent-2411-100: The RAMP algorithm follows four key steps.
elements.sent-2411-101: First, current policy $\pi$ is used to (1) sample new experiences in the environment to measure the policy $\pi$'s current occupancy measure.

elements.sent-2411-103: Next, the instrinsic reward model is updated to better (2) estimate the new versus past experiences.
elements.sent-2411-104: In RAMP, the reward model is represented by a simple neural network $f_{\phi}$ which is the solution to a specific optimization problem.
elements.sent-2411-105: Two alternate measures of divergence are possible: the KL divergence and the Wasserstein distance.
elements.sent-2411-106: Thus, the RAMP algorithm has two versions: $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightblue {\text{KL }$, which maximizes Objective \ref{obj:kl , and $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightcoral {\mathcal{W }$, which maximizes Objective \ref{obj:wasserstein .}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-108: Alternatively, for $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightcoral {\mathcal{W }$, $f_{\phi}$ is obtained by solving the constrained optimization problem described in Equation \ref{eq:w_classification .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-109: The third step (3) maximizes the difference between the present and the past distributions.
elements.sent-2411-110: The reward models proposed by RAMP can be maximized using any RL method.
elements.sent-2411-111: This study uses the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm \cite{haarnoja2018soft for all experiments.}

elements.sent-2411-143: RAMP takes a different approach by incrementally improving the Shannon entropy of the agent's state distribution over time, rather than directly maximizing it.

elements.sent-2411-146: By focusing on incremental improvement, RAMP offers a promising solution for efficient exploration.
elements.sent-2411-147: In this section, we study the capacity of the RAMP algorithm to lead to exploration in complex environments.
elements.sent-2411-148: We begin with a qualitative analysis of the reward models used by RAMP to better characterize the objectives of RAMP.
elements.sent-2411-149: We then perform a quantitative analysis of the exploration obtained by RAMP, using coverage of the state space as the exploration metric.
elements.sent-2411-150: Finally, we analyze the ability of RAMP to explore when also using an additional, extrinsic, reward.
elements.sent-2411-151: The evaluation is conducted on three different sets of tasks, which are illustrated and further detailed in Appendix~\ref{ap:benchmarks .}

elements.sent-2411-211: This paper presents a novel exploration method in reinforcement learning aimed at maximizing the Shannon entropy of an agent's experience distribution.
elements.sent-2411-212: By reformulating this objective, we derive a new method, RAMP, that encourages the agent to explore by distancing itself from past experiences.
elements.sent-2411-213: We investigate the use of KL divergence and Wasserstein distance to characterize the differences between the agent's current distribution and its past distribution.
elements.sent-2411-214: We characterize two versions of RAMP, ($\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightcoral {\mathcal{W }$ and $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightblue {\text{KL }$), and compare them.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-215: We find that maximizing the Wasserstein distance between two distributions under the temporal distance intuitively results in a different behavior compared to maximizing the KL divergence.
elements.sent-2411-216: To maximize the Wassterstein distance, the agent must encounter states that are as far away as possible from the states already encountered.
elements.sent-2411-217: This leads to effective exploration and even the ability to reach high rewards.
elements.sent-2411-218: Our evaluations reveal that, in locomotion tasks, $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightcoral {\mathcal{W }$ enables highly efficient exploration by extensively exploring over the agent's position.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: In the presence of extrinsic reward, $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightblue {\text{KL }$ also demonstrates the ability to explore sufficiently to lead to rewarding behaviors.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-220: Beyond the use of an extrinsic reward, $\text{RAMP _{\textcolor{lightblue {\text{KL }$ could also be performed by measuring the KL divergence of a featurization of the state space, rather than the full state.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-221: This could lead to exploration of new behaviors, as demonstrated under the presence of an extrinsic reward.
elements.sent-2411-222: We aim to study such a featurization for high-dimensional state spaces in future work.
elements.sent-2411-223: In summary, this work reframes exploration in reinforcement learning as an iterative process of distinguishing the agent’s present behavior from its past experiences, introducing a novel approach with wide applicability.
elements.sent-2411-224: The RAMP algorithm offers a new framing of exploration that could be combined with other exploration strategies, such as skill-based or hierarchical exploration.
elements.sent-2411-225: This new perspective on exploration, driven by maximizing the divergence between successive distributions, has the potential to advance both theoretical insights and practical algorithms for exploration in reinforcement learning.. \bibliographystyle{arxiv .}

elements.sent-2411-282: Given a distribution $S$ over $\mathcal{S}$, a standard classification problem can be expressed as: \begin{equation}L(\phi) = \underset{s\sim S}{E} \left[ l(y(s), \sigma(f_{\phi}(s)))\right]\end{equation} where $y(s)$ assigns a label of 0 or 1 to any sample $s$, and $l$ represents a loss function which attains $0$ when $y(s) = \sigma(f_{\phi (s))$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Typically, $l$ is a Cross-Entropy loss, which can be retrieved via a Kullback-Leibler divergence between two Bernoulli distributions $\mathcal{B}$, parameterized respectively by $y(s)$ and $\sigma(f_{\phi (s))$: \begin{equation}L(\phi) = \underset{s\sim S}{E}\left[KL(B(y(s)) || B(\sigma(f_{\phi}(s))))\right] = \underset{s\sim S}{E}\left[y(s)\log\left(\frac{y(s)}{\sigma(f_{\phi}(s))}\right) + (1-y(s))\log\left(\frac{1-y(s)}{1-\sigma(f_{\phi}(s))}\right) \right] = \underset{s\sim S}{E}\left[\underbrace{H(B(y(s)) | B(\sigma(f_{\phi}(s))))}_{Cross-entropy} - \underbrace{H(B(y(s)))}_{Shannon entropy} \right] = \underset{s\sim S}{E}\left[\underbrace{H(B(y(s)) | B(\sigma(f_{\phi}(s))))}_{Cross-entropy} - \underbrace{H(B(y(s)))}_{cte} \right]\end{equation} In contrastive learning, the classification problem typically involves two random variables, $S_{+}$ and $S_{-}$, and the target function $y(s)$ defined as: \begin{equation}y(s) = \left\{  1 \: if \: s\sim S_{+} 0 \: if \: s\sim S_{-}  \right.\end{equation}.}}
